metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step to the Remediation specifying that the test.yml gate script must be rewritten to parse `path <ceiling>` rows and compare each allowlisted file's current LOC against its recorded ceiling, replacing the current `comm -23` full-path string comparison.
findings.finding: The plan proposes converting the allowlist to `path <max-lines>` rows, but the current gate's comparison logic (`comm -23` on sorted full-path strings) is fundamentally incompatible with a two-column format. The plan specifies what must happen (AC: 'test.yml fails when an allowlisted file grows past its ceiling') but never acknowledges that the `allowed=$(grep ... | sort); comm -23` pipeline must be replaced with a parse-and-compare loop. An implementer who edits only the allowlist format without updating the gate script produces a silently broken gate.
findings.location: Remediation section: 'The gate compares each allowlisted file against ITS OWN recorded ceiling'; AC: 'test.yml fails when an allowlisted file grows past its ceiling'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Remediation: 'Replace the `allowed / comm -23` logic in the Module-size gate with a loop that: (1) reads each `<path> <ceiling>` row from the allowlist, (2) measures current LOC with `wc -l`, (3) fails if LOC > ceiling. Non-allowlisted files keep the existing `awk $1 > 800` check.' Example skeleton: `while IFS=' ' read -r path ceiling; do loc=$(wc -l < "$path"); if [ "$loc" -gt "$ceiling" ]; then echo "::error::$path exceeds ceiling $ceiling (now $loc)"; exit 1; fi; done < <(grep -vE ... allowlist)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py` (`read_module_size_allowlist`, `test_every_allowlist_entry_is_a_real_file`, `test_allowlist_equals_computed_over_cap_set`) to parse the new `path <max-lines>` format — extracting the path token for file-existence and set-membership checks, and validating the ceiling column separately.
findings.finding: The plan proposes changing the allowlist format to 'path <max-lines>' rows but does not mention updating tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py, which has a read_module_size_allowlist() parser (lines 47–54) that splits on bare paths and then calls (REPO_ROOT / entry).is_file() on each entry. With the new format, 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py 850' would fail the is_file() check, and the set-equality against compute_over_cap_modules() (which returns bare paths) would also break. The plan's own AC ('Current allowlisted files pass at their pinned ceilings') cannot be satisfied without updating this existing parser.
findings.location: Remediation section / AC: 'Current allowlisted files pass at their pinned ceilings' vs tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py lines 47–86
findings.suggested_fix: In read_module_size_allowlist(), split each stripped line on whitespace and take token[0] as the path (and optionally token[1] as the ceiling). Add a separate assertion that each entry has exactly two tokens and that the second is a positive integer. test_allowlist_equals_computed_over_cap_set then compares {path for path, _ in parsed} against compute_over_cap_modules().

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the module-size gate in `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` (lines 154–165) to match the new `path <max-lines>` parsing logic, keeping it in sync with `test.yml`.
findings.finding: The module-size gate shell script is duplicated verbatim in .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml (lines 154–165), which the plan does not mention. The plan specifies updating only test.yml. If gerrit-verify.yaml is not updated to the new 'path <max-lines>' parsing logic, Gerrit's Verified gate will either silently pass all allowlisted files (the old bare-path awk/comm logic will never match a 'path N' line against an over-cap filename) or produce a format mismatch that defeats the ceiling enforcement on the Gerrit path.
findings.location: Remediation section ('Also drop the __init__.py … gate-visible') / .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml lines 154–165

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` (Module-size gate block, lines 151-165) as an explicit edit target alongside `test.yml`, since it contains an identical gate script that reads the same allowlist.
findings.finding: The plan names `.github/workflows/test.yml` as the only workflow file to update, but `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` contains an identical copy of the Module-size gate shell block (lines 151-165) that reads from the same allowlist. Any format change to the allowlist (bare path → `path <max-lines>`) or to the gate's `awk`/`comm` logic will break the Gerrit Verified gate if `gerrit-verify.yaml` is not updated in lockstep.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the edit-target list: '`.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` — update the Module-size gate block (lines ~151-165) with the same `awk`/`comm` logic changes applied to `test.yml`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Remediation step specifying how `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py` must be updated to parse the new `path <max-lines>` format: `read_module_size_allowlist()` must extract only the path token, `test_every_allowlist_entry_is_a_real_file` must strip the ceiling suffix before the `is_file()` check, and `test_allowlist_equals_computed_over_cap_set` must compare bare paths (not `path N` strings) against `compute_over_cap_modules()`.
findings.finding: The plan converts the allowlist to a `path <max-lines>` format and updates the shell gate in `test.yml`, but never co-locates the required update to the Python-side processing protocol in `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`. `read_module_size_allowlist()` (lines 47–54) treats the entire stripped line as a file path and calls `(REPO_ROOT / entry).is_file()` — under the new format `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py 850` is not a valid file path, breaking `test_every_allowlist_entry_is_a_real_file`. Simultaneously, `test_allowlist_equals_computed_over_cap_set` compares the allowed set (now `{"src/… 850", …}`) against `compute_over_cap_modules()` (which returns bare POSIX paths), so the set-equality assertion will always fail. The plan's own AC-4 ('Current allowlisted files pass at their pinned ceilings') is structurally impossible without updating this parser.
findings.location: Remediation section ('Convert the allowlist from bare filenames to `path <max-lines>` rows') / AC-4 / tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py lines 47–86
findings.suggested_fix: In the Remediation section, add: 'Update `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`: `read_module_size_allowlist()` must parse `path ceiling` rows by splitting on whitespace and taking `tokens[0]` as the path (ignoring the ceiling token); `test_allowlist_equals_computed_over_cap_set` compares the extracted paths against `compute_over_cap_modules()`; add a new test `test_allowlist_ceilings_not_exceeded` that reads each `(path, ceiling)` pair and asserts `wc_l(path) <= ceiling`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's description: the allowlist already uses full relative paths (e.g., `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py`), not bare filenames — update the Remediation section to say 'Convert the existing full-path rows to `<full-path> <max-lines>` format'.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the allowlist contains 'bare filenames' (e.g., 'attest.py'), but the actual allowlist file uses full relative paths (e.g., 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py'), which is also what `find src/rebar -name '*.py'` produces. The `comm -23` comparison therefore works on full paths, not bare filenames. The remediation spec ('Convert the allowlist from bare filenames to path <max-lines> rows') inherits this false description of the existing format.
findings.location: Plan 'Finding (S1)' section: 'bare filenames'; Plan 'Remediation' section: 'Convert the allowlist from bare filenames to path <max-lines> rows'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'Convert the allowlist from bare filenames to path <max-lines> rows' to 'Convert the allowlist from bare-path rows (e.g., src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py) to `<path> <max-lines>` rows (e.g., src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py 837)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or correct the false premise: the gate has NO `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption (only `__pycache__` is excluded); revise the Remediation and AC to remove the 'drop the `__init__.py` exemption' step, or replace it with an accurate description of what change is needed for any `__init__.py` files currently over 800 LOC.
findings.finding: The plan asserts there is a '`__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption' in the gate that makes `__init__.py` files 'gate-invisible', and prescribes a remediation step to 'drop' it. No such exemption exists: the gate's only `grep -v` is `grep -v __pycache__`, which filters out cache directories, not `__init__.py` files. `__init__.py` files are fully subject to the 800-LOC gate. The `plan_review/__init__.py` is 508 lines (under cap) and has never needed exemption.
findings.location: Plan 'Remediation' section: 'Also drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption (give it a pinned ceiling row instead) so it is gate-visible'; AC line: '`__init__.py` is gate-visible (pinned row, not filename-exempt)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the hallucinated claim that a `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption exists in the gate; verify whether any `__init__.py` files currently exceed 800 LOC and, if so, add them to the scope as genuine missing allowlist entries.
findings.finding: The plan's Remediation section states 'drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption (give it a pinned ceiling row instead) so it is gate-visible,' implying the current gate has a special `__init__.py` bypass. No such exemption exists anywhere in `.github/workflows/test.yml` or `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` — the gate has no `__init__.py`-specific handling. This is a hallucinated edit target that would mislead an implementer.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Also drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption (give it a pinned ceiling row instead) so it is gate-visible' with a fact-based statement: check whether any `__init__.py` files exceed 800 LOC (e.g. `find src/rebar -name '__init__.py' | xargs wc -l | awk '$1>800'`) and if so, add them as explicit ceiling rows in the new format.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the hallucinated `__init__.py` filename-pattern-exemption claim from the Remediation section; replace it with a fact-grounded statement that explicitly checks whether any `__init__.py` files in `src/rebar` exceed 800 LOC (e.g., `src/rebar/__init__.py`) and, if so, requires their inclusion as `path <ceiling>` rows in the updated allowlist.
findings.finding: The plan's Remediation section instructs the executor to 'drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption (give it a pinned ceiling row instead) so it is gate-visible.' Tool-verified search of `.github/workflows/test.yml` and the entire `.github/` directory for `__init__` returns zero matches in gate logic. The gate is `find src/rebar -name '*.py' | grep -v __pycache__ | xargs wc -l | awk '$2 != "total" && $1 > 800 { print $2 }'` with no `__init__.py` special-case. This is a pink-elephant antipattern: the executor is instructed to remove a non-existent exemption, and AC-3 ('`__init__.py` is gate-visible (pinned row, not filename-exempt)') would then pass vacuously since `__init__.py` files were never exempted, masking whether any real `__init__.py` over-cap files need ceiling rows.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Also drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption' / AC-3
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Also drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption (give it a pinned ceiling row instead) so it is gate-visible' with: 'Verify which `__init__.py` files in `src/rebar` currently exceed 800 LOC (e.g., run `find src/rebar -name __init__.py | xargs wc -l | awk \'$1>800\'`); add each as a `path <current-LOC>` row in the updated allowlist — they are not currently exempted, they simply were not over cap at allowlist creation time.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Remediation section whether per-file ceilings should be set to (a) conversion-time LOC (current actual size) or (b) original allowlisting LOC, and resolve the contradiction with AC4.
findings.finding: 'ceiling = current LOC at grandfathering' is ambiguous: 'current' could mean the LOC when each file was originally allowlisted (e.g., attest.py at 268) or the LOC at the time this plan is executed (attest.py at 837). Given the files grew post-allowlisting, these values differ substantially, and AC4 ('current allowlisted files pass at their pinned ceilings') is only satisfiable if ceilings are set to conversion-time LOC — but the phrase 'at grandfathering' implies historical LOC. An agent can't resolve this without a clarification or stated default.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'ceiling = current LOC at grandfathering'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'ceiling = current LOC at grandfathering' with 'ceiling = LOC at the time this conversion is applied (i.e., today's measured line count for each file)' to make AC4 unambiguously satisfiable and give the executor a deterministic instruction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile Remediation and AC4: explicitly state whether the per-file ceiling is (a) the LOC at original grandfathering, (b) today's LOC at time of this change, or (c) some other value — and update AC4 to match that definition.
findings.finding: Contradiction between Remediation and AC4 on what 'ceiling' value to use: Remediation says 'ceiling = current LOC at grandfathering' (i.e., the historical LOC when the file was first allowlisted), but AC4 says 'current allowlisted files pass at their pinned ceilings', implying ceilings are set to today's LOC. For attest.py (grandfathering LOC ~268, today ~837), these two directives are irreconcilable — an implementer following Remediation literally would produce a ceiling that immediately fails AC4.
findings.location: Remediation section ('ceiling = current LOC at grandfathering') vs AC4 ('Current allowlisted files pass at their pinned ceilings')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'ceiling = current LOC at grandfathering' with 'ceiling = LOC at time this plan is implemented (i.e., today's measured line count)' and update AC4 to read 'Current allowlisted files pass at their recorded ceilings (today's measured LOC)'. Add a note that the ratchet prevents FUTURE growth; past growth is grandfathered at today's watermark.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for AC2 that exercises the gate end-to-end: e.g., temporarily add a synthetic allowlist row with a ceiling below the file's actual LOC, run the gate script locally, and assert non-zero exit; then restore and assert zero exit for the passing case.
findings.finding: AC2 ('test.yml fails when an allowlisted file grows past its ceiling') has no proving command that actually exercises the gate firing. The plan describes no step that temporarily inflates an allowlisted file (or crafts a synthetic fixture) to confirm CI rejects it. Without this, the gate could be written incorrectly and AC2 would be unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'test.yml fails when an allowlisted file grows past its ceiling; shrinking is free'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'Proving command: echo "src/fake.py 5" >> .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && printf "%s\n" {1..10} > src/fake.py && bash .github/check-module-size.sh; assert exit code 1. Then remove the row and assert exit 0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit atomicity requirement (both files changed in one commit/PR) and a one-line rollback note (revert that single commit to restore prior behavior) to the Remediation section.
findings.finding: The plan changes both the allowlist file format and the test.yml gate logic without specifying that these two changes must be applied atomically, nor providing a rollback path if the migration is applied partially (e.g., allowlist updated but test.yml not yet, or vice versa).
findings.location: Remediation section; Acceptance Criteria (AC1 and AC2 together imply a two-file change)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Remediation: 'Both `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` and `.github/workflows/test.yml` MUST be updated in a single atomic commit. To roll back, revert that commit; the prior bare-filename allowlist and 800-line gate are fully restored.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation artifact (inline allowlist header comment, CLAUDE.md entry, or README section) explaining the `path <max-lines>` row format, the ceiling-derivation rule (current LOC at grandfathering), and the protocol for legitimately bumping a ceiling.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel allowlist format (`path <max-lines>`) and a ceiling-bumping protocol ('a reviewed act') but specifies no documentation update — no ADR, no CLAUDE.md entry, no README section, and no inline comments in the allowlist or workflow explaining the new row format, the ceiling-derivation rule, or the procedure for legitimately raising a ceiling.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a multi-line comment block at the top of `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` (e.g., `# Format: <relative-path> <max-lines>\n# Ceiling = LOC at time of grandfathering. To raise: update the number in a reviewed PR.`) and reference it from a brief note in CLAUDE.md or the repo's contributing guide.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a retirement/cleanup rule for allowlist rows when their corresponding file is deleted or renamed (e.g., CI warns on rows with no matching file, or the PR author must remove the row).
findings.finding: The allowlist file's retirement lifecycle is unspecified: when an allowlisted file is deleted or renamed, its row remains in the allowlist indefinitely, accumulating dead entries. Over time this makes the allowlist misleading and harder to audit.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or CI check: after parsing the allowlist, verify each listed path exists in the repo; emit a warning (or fail) for rows with no matching file. This keeps the allowlist self-pruning.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an enforcement mechanism for ceiling bumps (e.g., CODEOWNERS requiring a named owner's approval for allowlist changes, or a mandatory PR label) so 'reviewed act' is technically enforced, not just socially expected.
findings.finding: The plan describes bumping a ceiling row as 'a reviewed act' but provides no enforcement mechanism — any contributor can bump a ceiling in a PR without any gate distinguishing a legitimate increase from an accidental one. The ownership and governance of ceiling updates is undefined.
findings.location: Remediation section: 'bump the row — a reviewed act'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a CODEOWNERS entry for `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` requiring approval from a named maintainer group, and document this in the plan's Remediation section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC#3: the current gate has no `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption to drop; replace this AC with an explicit requirement that every currently-over-800 file (including `src/rebar/__init__.py` if not already in the allowlist) receives a pinned `path <max-lines>` row at its current LOC.
findings.finding: The plan claims the current gate has a `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption that needs to be dropped, but this exemption does not exist in the current code. The gate in test.yml (line 183) filters only `grep -v __pycache__`; `__init__.py` files appear in the allowlist as full paths (e.g., `src/rebar/__init__.py`) and are already gate-visible. An implementer looking for a `grep -v __init__` pattern to remove will find none, and the AC as written refers to a non-existent artefact.
findings.location: Remediation section: 'drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption'; AC line: '`__init__.py` is gate-visible (pinned row, not filename-exempt)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC line '`__init__.py` is gate-visible (pinned row, not filename-exempt)' with: '`src/rebar/__init__.py` and all other currently-over-800 files each have a `path <max-lines>` row in the allowlist pinned at their LOC at the time of this change.' Remove the 'drop filename-pattern exemption' language from the Remediation section as it references a non-existent mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the Remediation section explaining why `path <max-lines>` inline format was chosen (e.g., 'keeps the gate self-contained in pure shell with no new dependencies, consistent with the existing gate's design constraint of running on every OS without Python').
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for the `path <max-lines>` inline format over alternatives (e.g., a separate JSON/TOML ceiling registry, a Python helper script invoked from the gate step). The plan's 'Finding' section documents the problem well but the Remediation section jumps to implementation without explaining why the chosen format is the right fit for this codebase.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
